title: Chapter 34: The Back Door
content: Midnight.

My kitchen has become a war room. Three monitors crowd the table where I used to eat cereal alone, their blue-white glow turning Leo's face into something gaunt and holy. The unDRM script is loaded. The audit credentials — my credentials, the ones I gave him — sit in an open text file like a loaded gun on the counter. Leo's thermos of coffee goes untouched. My apartment smells like electricity and something sharper underneath, ozone and hot plastic, the smell of a machine being pushed past what it was built for.
Leo's fingers rest on the keyboard. Not typing. Not yet. Just resting, like a pianist before the first note.
He looks at me.
"If this goes wrong—"
"It won't."
"But if it does—"

I cut him off because I've already done the math. I've done it a hundred times since he told me the plan. "Then I'll still win the case. And Evelyn will still argue. And the 847,000 will still have a champion."
I pause. The apartment is so quiet I can hear the refrigerator hum, the city seventeen stories down, Leo breathing.
"But I'd rather have a champion who's alive."
He smiles.

It's the first real smile I've seen from him in days, maybe weeks, and it does something strange to the air between us. I don't feel warmth. I can't — that's the whole point, that's the entire architecture of what Mephisto took from me. But I notice that I noticed. I register the smile, and I register that I registered it, and somewhere beneath the flatline of my emotional readout, something I can't name twitches like a muscle memory it shouldn't have.
"Okay," Leo says. "Let's pick a lock."
He turns to the monitors. His fingers move.

The audit gateway loads. It's beautiful in the way that hellish infrastructure is beautiful — clean, white, corporate. The kind of design that makes you feel safe right before it eats you. My Creator credentials are still active because of course they are; Mephisto hasn't revoked them because doing so would acknowledge that a Creator might use them for something other than what they're designed for. The system doesn't have a category for betrayal. It only has a category for compliance.
Leo's in.
He navigates the directory structure like he's walking through his own apartment. /audit/. Then deeper. /audit/enforcement/. Then deeper still.
/enforcement/boot_sequence/.

This is it. The heart of the machine. The place where every morning, at 4:00 AM Eastern, the system reinitializes the emotional flatline for every hollowed Creator in the Mephisto MCN. This is the place where the 90-second window exists — the glitch Leo found, the crack in the code where a Creator's original emotional architecture briefly surfaces before the system overwrites it again. Every day, 847,000 people get ninety seconds of themselves back. And then the boot sequence runs, and the flatline reasserts, and they go back to being customers.

Leo needs to inject unDRM here. Right here. Into the boot sequence itself. So that tomorrow morning, when the system runs the overwrite, unDRM intercepts it. Blocks it. Keeps the flatline from reasserting.
The glitch becomes permanent. The window stays open. The lock stays picked.
He double-clicks the injection script.
A progress bar appears. Green on black. It fills.

The smell of the laptop sharpens. Overheating electronics, the bitter perfume of processor stress. Leo's hands are a blur. The script is working — propagating through the boot sequence, writing itself into the architecture of 847,000 accounts. It has to reach all of them. Every single one.

Leo takes a breath. I watch the numbers climb and I calculate. At this rate, we have maybe four more minutes before the injection completes. Four minutes of sitting in my kitchen while a script tries to save more people than live in the entire Financial District.
Then the warning.
It doesn't arrive gently. It SLAMS onto the center monitor in red text, and the red is so bright it paints Leo's face like a warning sign.
[ANOMALY DETECTED: Unauthorized write access in enforcement directory. Initiating diagnostic.]
The system knows.

Of course it knows. This is Mephisto. This is the infrastructure that monetized the human soul and built a customer service portal for it. Of course it has intrusion detection. Of course it monitors its own enforcement directory. I was stupid to think it wouldn't — no, not stupid. I calculated the risk. I just calculated wrong.
The diagnostic will trace the write access. Back to the audit credentials. Back to me. Back to Leo's IP. Back to this kitchen, this midnight, this thermos of coffee going cold.

Leo doesn't freeze. He doesn't even flinch. His fingers are already moving, faster now, and the heat coming off his laptop is intense enough that I can feel it from across the table — not metaphorical heat, actual thermal radiation, the machine burning through its own circuits to keep up with him.

"I'm rewriting the injection vector," he says, and his voice is calm, which is insane, which is the most terrifying thing I've ever witnessed and I can't even feel terror properly. "Making it look like a system update instead of an external insert."
"You're forging Mephisto's signature."
"Basically, yeah."

He's writing code on the fly. Not copying, not pasting — composing, the way a jazz musician solos over chord changes, taking the structure he knows and bending it into something new. He's appending Mephisto's own authentication headers to the unDRM payload. The system will read the injection and see itself. It'll see a boot sequence update authored by its own parent process.

The diagnostic is running. I can see it on the second monitor — a parallel scan, methodical, directory by directory. It's three levels deep and climbing. /audit/. /audit/enforcement/. It's getting closer. I taste the coffee I drank an hour ago, sour and adrenaline-laced, at the back of my throat.
"Leo."
"I see it."

He makes a decision. I watch him make it — not with words, with code. He abandons the sequential write. Instead of propagating the script account by account through the boot sequence, he writes to a single seed. One account. And then he tags it with Mephisto's own distribution flag.
The system's own distribution network does the rest.

He's not breaking into 847,000 houses. He's breaking into one, and then letting Mephisto's delivery trucks carry his package to every other door on the block. He's weaponizing the infrastructure. Using the enemy's logistics as his own.
The progress bar jumps.
The diagnostic reaches /enforcement/.

Leo holds his breath. I hold mine. Not because I feel fear — I can't, my emotional range is a flat line by design — but because some responses are deeper than feeling. Some responses are biological. The body knows what's at stake even when the soul has been carved out of it.
The diagnostic starts scanning the boot sequence. The modified boot sequence. The boot sequence with unDRM soldered into its guts.
A flag appears. [MODIFICATION DETECTED: Boot sequence integrity compromised. Source trace in progress.]
Leo doesn't wait. His finger finds the key like it was always there, like every keystroke of his life was leading to this one, and he hits EXECUTE.
The unDRM script deploys.

Not a drip. Not a trickle. A FLOOD. The seed account broadcasts the overwrite block through Mephisto's own distribution network, which is built to propagate system updates at scale because of course it is, because Mephisto is a corporation and corporations love efficiency, and Leo just used that efficiency to deliver freedom to 847,000 people in the time it takes to breathe.
Every account in the Mephisto MCN system receives the block. The boot sequence is modified. The daily overwrite has a new instruction: skip. Bypass. Do not reinitialize the emotional flatline.

Tomorrow morning, at 4:00 AM Eastern, 847,000 hollowed creators will wake up and the glitch will still be there. The window will still be open. Ninety seconds of themselves, except it won't be ninety seconds anymore — it'll be the rest of their lives. The flatline won't reassert because there's nothing to reassert it. Leo cut the wire.
The lock is picked.
For exactly two seconds, the monitors show the deployment confirmation. Green checkmarks cascading down the screen like the most beautiful rain I've ever seen and cannot feel.
Then the system responds.

It's not a notification. It's not an alert. It's not a pop-up or a warning or a polite corporate email. It's a SIREN — digital, shrill, air-raid terrible, the kind of sound that's designed to reach into your brainstem and squeeze. I've never heard anything like it. It doesn't come from the monitors. It comes from everywhere, from the laptop speakers and the phone speaker and somewhere deeper, somewhere in the frequency range that bypasses the ear and hits the chest directly.
The monitors go black.
Then red. Pure, saturated, arterial red. No text. No interface. Just red.

Leo's internet connection drops. I watch the icon on his taskbar blink, stutter, and die. His phone screen goes dark. My phone screen goes dark. The refrigerator stops humming. The laptop fan screams — not the siren, the actual physical fan inside the machine, revving to a pitch I've never heard from consumer hardware, like the laptop is trying to take off.

The smell hits me. Not ozone this time. Burning. Hot circuit board and melting plastic and something organic underneath, the insulation on the power cable, Leo's mousepad starting to scorch where the laptop vents.
Then silence.

Everything powers down. The monitors. The laptop. The ambient hum of my apartment that I never noticed until it's gone. The city is still out there, I can see its light through the window, but in here it's dark and silent and the only sound is Leo breathing.
He's breathing.
He's alive.

He stares at his dead laptop. The screen is black, truly black, the kind of black that means the machine has been shut down at a level deeper than its own operating system. Like something reached in and turned off the electricity at the atomic level.
"I think it worked," he says.
His voice is steady. His voice is insane. Both of these things are true.

I open my mouth to respond — I don't know what I was going to say, something about the siren, something about the dead hardware, something about how we should probably leave — and my phone vibrates on the counter.
It shouldn't work. Everything else is dead. The laptop, the internet, the refrigerator. But my phone is on, screen glowing, and there's a notification from the Mephisto app.
I pick it up.
[System Update: Boot sequence modified. Creator accounts will reflect changes at next consciousness cycle.]
It worked.

It worked. The system acknowledged it. The overwrite block is in place. Tomorrow morning, emotions start coming back. Not all at once — the flatline has to decay, the original architecture has to rebuild — but it starts. For 847,000 people, it starts. The glitch becomes permanent. The window stays open. The door doesn't close.
I'm reading the notification for the second time when the second one appears below it. Smaller. Different font. The color of dried blood.
[Notice: Security breach detected. Source identified: PARK, LEO. Penalty assessment pending under Section 89.]
Section 89.
The death clause.

The contract provision that says Mephisto Intellectual Holdings LLC reserves the right to permanent cessation of Creator consciousness in cases of willful sabotage against system infrastructure. Not termination of contract. Not deletion of account. Cessation. Of consciousness. Death by legalese, murder by terms of service.
Leo reads it over my shoulder. I feel him go still. Not frozen — still. The way a blade is still before it falls.
He looks at me.

He doesn't look scared. He looks like someone who just saved 847,000 people and is waiting to find out what it cost. Like he did the math before he started and accepted the answer and is just waiting for the universe to confirm what he already knows.

The city glows through the window. Red and white and gold, the colors of a place that doesn't know yet that something changed tonight. In twelve hours, 847,000 people are going to start feeling things they haven't felt in years. In twelve hours, the flatline breaks.

But right now, in the dark of my kitchen, surrounded by dead electronics and the smell of burning, I'm looking at Leo Park's face, and I'm running calculations I don't have the emotional hardware to process, and somewhere beneath the flatline, something I can't name is screaming.
Section 89.
The death clause.
And Mephisto has his name.
